data.title: Four Days Left To Buy Italian Wine Brands S.p.A. (BIT:IWB) Before The Ex-Dividend Date
data.description:
It looks like Italian Wine Brands S.p.A. (BIT:IWB) is about to go ex-dividend in the next 4 days. The ex-dividend date generally occurs two days before the record date, which is the day on which shareholders need to be on the company's books in order to receive a dividend. The ex-dividend date is of consequence because whenever a stock is bought or sold, the trade can take two business days or more to settle. Thus, you can purchase Italian Wine Brands' shares before the 4th of May in order to receive the dividend, which the company will pay on the 6th of May.

The company's next dividend payment will be €0.50 per share, and in the last 12 months, the company paid a total of €0.50 per share. Calculating the last year's worth of payments shows that Italian Wine Brands has a trailing yield of 2.5% on the current share price of €19.86. If you buy this business for its dividend, you should have an idea of whether Italian Wine Brands's dividend is reliable and sustainable. That's why we should always check whether the dividend payments appear sustainable, and if the company is growing.

Trump has pledged to "unleash" American oil and gas and these 15 US stocks have developments that are poised to benefit.

If a company pays out more in dividends than it earned, then the dividend might become unsustainable - hardly an ideal situation. Italian Wine Brands paid out a comfortable 28% of its profit last year. Yet cash flows are even more important than profits for assessing a dividend, so we need to see if the company generated enough cash to pay its distribution. What's good is that dividends were well covered by free cash flow, with the company paying out 23% of its cash flow last year.

It's positive to see that Italian Wine Brands's dividend is covered by both profits and cash flow, since this is generally a sign that the dividend is sustainable, and a lower payout ratio usually suggests a greater margin of safety before the dividend gets cut.

View our latest analysis for Italian Wine Brands

Click here to see the company's payout ratio, plus analyst estimates of its future dividends.

Advertisement

Have Earnings And Dividends Been Growing?

Stocks with flat earnings can still be attractive dividend payers, but it is important to be more conservative with your approach and demand a greater margin for safety when it comes to dividend sustainability. If business enters a downturn and the dividend is cut, the company could see its value fall precipitously. With that in mind, we're not enthused to see that Italian Wine Brands's earnings per share have remained effectively flat over the past five years. We'd take that over an earnings decline any day, but in the long run, the best dividend stocks all grow their earnings per share.

The main way most investors will assess a company's dividend prospects is by checking the historical rate of dividend growth. Italian Wine Brands has delivered an average of 2.8% per year annual increase in its dividend, based on the past eight years of dividend payments.

To Sum It Up

Is Italian Wine Brands worth buying for its dividend? Earnings per share have been flat, although at least the company is paying out a low and conservative percentage of both its earnings and cash flow. It's definitely not great to see earnings falling, but at least there may be some buffer before the dividend gets cut. In summary, while it has some positive characteristics, we're not inclined to race out and buy Italian Wine Brands today.

data.title: UPS (UPS) Margin Compression In Q1 2026 Challenges Bullish Earnings Recovery Narratives
data.description:

The company has seen quarterly revenue move from US$25.3b in Q4 2024 to US$21.5b in Q1 2025 and US$21.2b in Q1 2026, while basic EPS over those same periods has gone from US$2.02 to US$1.40 and then to US$1.02. This puts more focus on how efficiently UPS converts its top line into profit as margins come under closer scrutiny.

  • Consensus narrative expects margin improvement over time, yet the recent slip from 6.4% to 5.9% means:
    • Quarterly net income moved from US$1.8b in Q4 2025 to US$864m in Q1 2026, while revenue went from US$24.5b to US$21.2b, so the margin compression in the trailing figures lines up with softer recent profitability.

Some investors see this margin picture as a temporary step on the way to better profitability, while others focus more on the recent compression when weighing the consensus narrative around UPS.

    • The P/E discount to peers fits the bullish framing of relative value, but the high debt load and a dividend yield of 6.31% that is not well covered by free cash flow show why the stock might not trade closer to the DCF figure right now.

Bulls see the valuation gap and lower P/E as an opportunity, while the weaker earnings trend and funding mix are key reasons others stay more cautious about how quickly that gap might close.

Approach 1: PDD Holdings Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model projects a company’s future cash flows and then discounts them back to today’s money to estimate what the entire business could be worth right now.

For PDD Holdings, Simply Wall St uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model based on cash flows in CN¥. The latest twelve month free cash flow is CN¥106,058.87m, just over CN¥106.1b. Analyst estimates and subsequent extrapolations project free cash flow of CN¥117,888.57m in 2035, or about CN¥117.9b, with interim years ranging between roughly CN¥119.3b and CN¥173.6b according to the provided forecast path.

Discounting these future CN¥ cash flows back to today results in an estimated intrinsic value of US$179.09 per share. Compared with the current share price of US$97.29, the model implies a 45.7% discount, indicating that, on this cash flow view alone, the stock screens as undervalued.

data.title: Rogers Voluntary Buyouts Reshape Cost Base And Investor Risk Reward Profile
data.description:
• Rogers Communications has launched one of the largest rounds of voluntary buyouts in Canadian telecom history.
  • The program offers departure and retirement packages to roughly half of its workforce.
  • The move is part of a wide cost cutting effort that follows earlier capital expenditure reductions.

For investors watching TSX:RCI.B, this decision comes with the share price at about CA$49.33 and a mixed recent track record. The stock is up 9.4% over the past week and 44.9% over the past year, while the 3 year and 5 year returns show declines of 17.0% and 2.0%. That split performance highlights how sensitive Rogers can be to large operational shifts such as this one.

The scale of the buyout offer points to a meaningful reset in how Rogers approaches its cost base and staffing model. As details emerge on uptake rates and any resulting restructuring charges, investors can watch how management balances lower operating costs with service quality, network performance, and longer term strategic priorities.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Rogers Communications by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Rogers Communications.

5 things going right for Rogers Communications that this headline doesn't cover.

The voluntary buyout program sits on top of a cost story that was already shifting. Rogers has just reported Q1 2026 sales of CA$5,482m and net income of CA$438m, alongside a CA$0.50 quarterly dividend declaration, so the company is not reacting from a position of obvious distress. Instead, the buyouts line up with the previously flagged plan to cut 2026 capital spending by roughly 30% versus 2025. Together, lower capex and a leaner payroll could support higher free cash generation and give Rogers more room to manage its debt load and continue funding network and satellite-to-mobile initiatives that compete with BCE and Telus.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Rogers Communications Narrative

  • The focus on a leaner cost base supports the narrative theme of cost efficiencies and reduced capital intensity helping margins and cash generation.
  • If buyouts dilute service quality, slow integration work, or disrupt sports and media operations, they could challenge expectations that execution on Shaw integration and MLSE will be smooth.
  • The narrative highlights cost savings but does not explicitly account for large scale voluntary programs where take up rates, restructuring charges, and cultural impact may differ from earlier assumptions.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Rogers Communications to help decide what it is worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • Execution risk if a large portion of experienced staff exits at once, especially in network operations and customer service where service issues could hurt churn and pricing power.
  • Analysts already flag high leverage and interest coverage as key risks, so if short term restructuring costs are large, they could narrow the buffer on interest payments.
  • Potential for a structurally lower cost base that supports earnings and free cash flow, alongside Q1 profitability and the continued CA$0.50 quarterly dividend.
  • Ability to redirect savings into areas like satellite-to-mobile coverage and 5G expansion, which can help Rogers compete with BCE and Telus on coverage and product breadth.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, the key questions are how many employees accept the buyouts, how large any one off restructuring charges are, and whether service metrics or churn move in response. Investors can also monitor whether management links the program to explicit targets for operating expense savings or leverage. Progress on the previously outlined capex reductions, together with any updates on satellite-to-mobile adoption and MLSE consolidation, will help show whether Rogers is using its slimmer cost base to support long term network and media priorities rather than just short term margin optics.

data.title: Oracle’s OpenAI Concerns Put Massive AI Infrastructure Plans Under Scrutiny
data.description:
• Oracle (NYSE:ORCL) faces rising concern over its large AI infrastructure commitments tied to OpenAI.
  • Reports indicate OpenAI has missed internal user and revenue targets for 2026, putting long term obligations under the spotlight.
  • The news has coincided with a sharp sell off in AI infrastructure linked stocks and a broader reassessment of hyperscaler risk exposure.

For you as an Oracle watcher, the tension sits squarely in Oracle's AI story, where OpenAI is a flagship partner for massive, multi year data center and infrastructure deals. Oracle has leaned into AI workloads as a key use case for its cloud infrastructure, so any questions around OpenAI's demand profile or contract durability naturally pull focus to this part of the business.

From here, the key questions are less about immediate headlines and more about how resilient these commitments prove if AI usage patterns change or scale more slowly than planned. Investors will likely be watching for clearer disclosures on contract structures, off ramp provisions, and how diversified Oracle's AI customer base is beyond OpenAI.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Oracle by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Oracle.

See which insiders are buying and buying and selling Oracle following this latest news.

For investors, the OpenAI headlines have collided with Oracle’s already heavy AI infrastructure spending, including large data center projects and sizeable contract obligations. The sell off in Oracle and other AI infrastructure names signals that the market is reassessing how much risk sits in long term, partner specific commitments when one of the flagship customers is reported to be missing internal growth targets. At the same time, Oracle has been adding AI related clients, multicloud partnerships with Google Cloud and AWS, and new data center capacity through suppliers such as Datapod and projects like Michigan and Project Jupiter in New Mexico. The tension for you is between those broad AI and cloud demand signals and the concentration risk that comes from a very large deal with a single AI lab.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Oracle Narrative

  • The concerns around OpenAI’s growth directly tie into the narrative’s point that Oracle is supplying AI workloads for major customers such as OpenAI, Meta and xAI, and that large remaining performance obligations depend on continued appetite for AI compute.
  • The report that OpenAI may struggle to fund future computing contracts challenges the narrative’s assumption that contracted AI demand converts smoothly into revenue, especially when Oracle is raising US$45b to US$50b for data center expansion and carrying negative free cash flow.
  • The narrative focuses on broad AI and multicloud demand but does not fully factor in how credit markets and equity investors might reprice risk if one cornerstone customer needs to renegotiate or delay a US$300b infrastructure deal.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Oracle to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Analysts have flagged that Oracle’s debt is not well covered by operating cash flow, and a very large AI buildout tied partly to OpenAI adds another layer of execution and funding risk if AI workloads scale more slowly than expected.
  • ⚠️ Heavy reliance on a small set of hyperscale and AI customers such as OpenAI, Microsoft, Amazon and others could become a problem if any of them trim data center commitments or shift AI workloads to rivals like AWS, Microsoft Azure or Google Cloud.
  • 🎁 Oracle’s broader AI positioning, including the AI Database Agent with Google Cloud, expanded multicloud networking with AWS and a global modular data center deal with Datapod, points to efforts to diversify AI related revenue beyond a single partner.
  • 🎁 Some investors view Oracle as trading below one estimate of fair value and note that earnings are forecast to grow and have recently grown, which, if sustained alongside high utilization of new AI campuses, could help support the long term investment case.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, it is worth watching for any fresh disclosure on the size, timing and terms of Oracle’s OpenAI related contracts, including off ramp clauses or usage based provisions. Pay attention to how credit spreads and debt terms evolve for Oracle’s project financings, and whether management gives more color on customer diversification across AI, database and multicloud deals. Comparing Oracle’s AI capital spending, utilization commentary and customer mix with peers such as Microsoft, Amazon and Alphabet can also help you gauge whether the market’s current caution on AI infrastructure is specific to Oracle’s partner exposure or part of a wider reset.

data.title: Wärtsilä Oyj Abp (HLSE:WRT1V) Net Margin Reaches 9.4% Testing Bullish Narratives
data.description:
Wärtsilä Oyj Abp (HLSE:WRT1V) opened 2026 with Q1 revenue of €1.6b and basic EPS of €0.25. This shows how its recent profit expansion is feeding through to both the top and bottom line. Over the past year, quarterly revenue has moved from €1.56b in Q1 2025 to a range of €1.63b to €2.00b, while quarterly EPS stepped from €0.21 in Q1 2025 to between roughly €0.23 and €0.32, with trailing twelve month EPS at about €1.10. With net income and margins having trended higher over the last year, this set of results keeps the focus squarely on how sustainably Wärtsilä can defend its profitability.

Advertisement

Margins Backed By 9.4% Net Profit Level

  • On a trailing twelve month basis, Wärtsilä earned €650 million of net income on €6.9b of revenue, which implies a 9.4% net margin compared with 8.1% a year earlier.
  • Supporters of the bullish view argue that margin expansion can continue, and the latest numbers give them some backing, but also some checks:
    • The move in trailing net income from €540 million to €650 million over the last year lines up with the reported 20.4% earnings growth, which fits the bullish idea that profits are scaling faster than sales.
    • At the same time, the bullish narrative talks about margins reaching around 9.4% in a few years, while the trailing figure is already at 9.4%. Future progress would therefore need to come from holding or improving that level rather than catching up to it.

Strong recent margin levels and earnings growth are exactly what bullish investors point to when they say Wärtsilä could keep turning higher revenue into solid profits, but the data also shows how much is already achieved, which makes future execution important for that story to hold. 🐂 Wärtsilä Oyj Abp Bull Case

Premium P/E Of 32.5x Versus Peers

  • Wärtsilä currently trades on a trailing P/E of 32.5x, compared with about 20.3x for the wider European Machinery group and 20.5x for direct peers, while a DCF fair value of €30.38 sits below the current share price of €35.91.
  • Bears highlight this valuation gap as their key concern, and the numbers illustrate why they focus on it:
    • Earnings grew 20.4% over the last year, yet the P/E is roughly 12 points above peer and industry averages, so a lot of that growth is already reflected in the price investors are paying today.
    • The difference between the share price of €35.91 and the DCF fair value of €30.38 suggests that any disappointment against the roughly 7.1% revenue and 8.3% earnings growth forecasts could matter quickly for a valuation that already sits above the 30.99 analyst target level.

The current valuation leaves little room for mistakes in the cautious view, which is why more bearish investors focus on the premium multiples and the gap between price, DCF fair value, and the 30.99 analyst target. 🐻 Wärtsilä Oyj Abp Bear Case

TTM EPS Tops €1.10 After Multi Year Expansion

  • Trailing twelve month basic EPS has reached about €1.10, up from €0.85 two years earlier, alongside five year earnings growth that averaged 44.3% per year and the most recent 12 month increase of 20.4%.
  • Analysts with a balanced view see this EPS history as both a strength and a reference point for moderating growth expectations:
    • The shift in trailing EPS from €0.85 to €1.10 and net income from €503 million to €650 million shows how quickly profitability has scaled, but consensus now looks for earnings growth of around 8.3% a year, which is well below the 44.3% five year pace.
    • That contrast between past growth and more moderate forecasts helps explain why analysts collectively sit at a 30.99 target while the market price is €35.91. This suggests they see the recent strong EPS run as hard to repeat at the same rate.
